nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of deep lagoons and seaward reefs, from the lower surge zone down to roughly 50 m; tends to be territorial and is generally observed close to rockwork and coral cover

decorPreferences: large rockwork and caves for retreat

diet.notes: The Undulate Triggerfish is a carnivore with a broad wild diet that includes echinoderms, molluscs, small fish, tunicates, sponges, hydrozoans, and some algae — reflecting a trophic level of roughly 3.4. In captivity, a varied meaty diet of squid, krill, clams, mussels, mysis shrimp, and whole hard-shelled shrimp or snails is widely recommended. The hard-shelled items serve a practical purpose: the species has continuously growing incisor-like teeth, and crunching through tough shells helps keep them worn down. Feeding once or twice daily tends to be sufficient.

goodWith: other large robust predators of similar size

summary: The Undulate Triggerfish (Balistapus undulatus) is a boldly patterned Indo-Pacific species with an emerald-green body and vivid yellow-orange stripes. Widely considered one of the most aggressive triggerfish in the hobby, it is broadly regarded as incompatible with reef systems and best kept in large species-only or predator fish-only setups.
careNotes: A species-only or large predator-only system is generally advised; reef compatibility is broadly considered out of the question, as the Undulate Triggerfish will consume invertebrates, crustaceans, molluscs, and corals, and is known to rearrange or dislodge rockwork. A minimum tank footprint of around 180 cm (roughly 700 L) is widely recommended given the species' documented aggression, full adult size of around 30 cm, and need for territory and open swimming space. The species produces audible grunting sounds when stressed or excited. Its incisor-like teeth grow continuously, so including hard-shelled food items — whole shrimp, clams, or snails — in the diet is generally advised to help wear them down and prevent overgrowth. Caution is broadly recommended when servicing the tank, as bites have been widely reported. Trade specimens are wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported. In the wild, spawning has been described as occurring in loose aggregations with nesting in reef channels; eggs are laid as a spongy cluster in a shallow excavation, hatch nocturnally, and are guarded by one or two adults. Visual differences between males and females are not well documented.

additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, taxonomy (Balistidae, Tetraodontiformes), max length 30 cm TL (common 26 cm), depth 0–60 m, wild temperature 25.1–29.1°C, native range across the Indo-Pacific (Red Sea to Tuamoto Is., S. Japan to GBR/New Caledonia), omnivorous benthic diet (trophic level 3.4), and wild reproduction (nesting in channels, eggs in spongy clusters, nest guarding by adults).

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (12 in / ~30 cm), temperature (72–79°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), and aggression notes. Reported min tank size of 75 gal — notably lower than LiveAquaria's 180 gal; this lower figure was not adopted given the species' documented adult size and aggression.

additional.name: ReefTrak
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 in), min tank (180 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.3), salinity (1.02–1.025), carnivorous diet, reef-incompatibility, and Indo-Pacific native range. Used as third-source confirmation after the designated Reef2Reef wiki link returned HTTP 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported. Wild observations describe channel-nesting with eggs deposited as a spongy cluster in a shallow excavation, hatched nocturnally, with nest-guarding by one or two adults.
